question: You've got practice in 4 hours. When does hydration start?
explanation: Champions show up already hydrated. Drink a couple of big glasses of water in the hours before training, then a glass right before you start. Start ahead, stay ahead.
fun_fact: Dehydration causes early fatigue — hydrating early is a real performance edge.

question: How often should you drink during exercise?
explanation: Keep the fluids flowing — a few big gulps every 15-20 minutes keeps your engine cool and your performance strong.
fun_fact: Even elite athletes schedule their sips — hydration is a skill you can train.

question: Your session is going longer than an hour. What's the smart fluid choice?
explanation: Water wins for shorter sessions. Past the one-hour mark, a sports beverage helps replace what you're sweating out and keeps your energy up.
fun_fact: Your body loses more than water when you sweat — that's where sports drinks earn their place.

question: How do you know how much fluid to replace after training?
option_b: Guess based on how you feel
explanation: Smart athletes track their sweat loss. Weigh in before and after — for every pound lost, drink a big glass or two of fluid within a couple hours. Simple, measurable, powerful.
fun_fact: Sweat loss is so individual that pro teams weigh every athlete before and after practice.

question: What's a proven recovery drink after a hard workout?
explanation: Chocolate milk is a recovery powerhouse — fluid to rehydrate plus carbs and protein to refuel. Tasty AND effective.
fun_fact: Chocolate milk has the carb-to-protein combo sports dietitians recommend after training.

question: What's the easiest way to check your hydration status?
option_a: Urine color — lighter means better hydrated
correct_answer: Urine color — lighter means better hydrated
explanation: Your body gives you a built-in hydration report. Light-colored urine means you're on track. Dark means drink up!
fun_fact: This check is used by athletes at every level, from high school to the Olympics.

question: What's sweat mostly made of?
explanation: Sweat is mostly water carrying electrolytes — sodium and chloride lead the way, with potassium, magnesium, and calcium in smaller amounts. Every one has a job.
fun_fact: A hard-training athlete can lose 1-3 liters of sweat per hour — that's up to a big soda bottle and a half!

question: Which electrolytes team up to control your fluid balance?
explanation: Sodium, chloride, and potassium work together to regulate and maintain fluid balance — the foundation of hydration.
fun_fact: Magnesium and calcium handle a different job: muscle function and energy metabolism.

question: When does electrolyte replacement matter MOST?
option_a: High-intensity training longer than an hour
correct_answer: High-intensity training longer than an hour
explanation: Long, intense, sweaty sessions are when electrolytes really count. Shorter workouts? Water and your regular meals have you covered.
fun_fact: That salty crust on your skin after hard training is literal proof of electrolyte loss.

question: Which foods help replace the sodium you sweat out?
explanation: Real food is a legit electrolyte source. Salted trail mix, pretzels, and crackers put sodium back where it belongs.
fun_fact: Sodium and chloride are the two most abundant electrolytes in your sweat.

question: You want potassium from food. What's on your plate?
explanation: Bananas, potatoes, dark leafy greens, and citrus fruits are potassium powerhouses that support fluid balance and muscle performance.
fun_fact: Potatoes pack even more potassium than bananas.

question: Which combo delivers magnesium for your muscles?
option_a: Pumpkin seeds, almonds, peanut butter, spinach, and beans
correct_answer: Pumpkin seeds, almonds, peanut butter, spinach, and beans
explanation: Magnesium keeps your muscles firing and your energy metabolism strong — and it's hiding in seeds, nuts, nut butters, spinach, and beans.
fun_fact: For calcium, reach for milk, yogurt, almonds, and broccoli — strong muscles AND strong bones.

question: How many components build a complete performance plate?
option_a: 5 — grains, lean protein, fruits/veggies, healthy fats, and fluids
correct_answer: 5 — grains, lean protein, fruits/veggies, healthy fats, and fluids
explanation: Five components, every meal: whole grains for energy, lean protein for muscle, fruits and veggies for protection, healthy fats for immunity and flavor, and fluids to stay hydrated. Build it and own it.
fun_fact: Sports dietitians call every meal a 'training table plate' — your plate is part of practice.

question: Hard training day. How much of your plate should be energy foods like whole grains?
explanation: Big work needs big fuel. On hard days: half your plate whole grains, a quarter lean protein, a quarter fruits and veggies — plus healthy fats and plenty of fluids.
fun_fact: Your calorie and carb needs peak on hard training days and in-season.

question: Light training day. How does your plate change?
option_a: Half the plate becomes fruits and veggies
correct_answer: Half the plate becomes fruits and veggies
explanation: Lighter day, lighter fuel: half your plate fruits and veggies, a quarter grains, a quarter lean protein. Matching your plate to your day is next-level fueling.
fun_fact: Adjusting your plate to training intensity is the same strategy college and pro athletes use.

question: Which of these counts as a lean, muscle-building protein?
option_a: Grilled chicken, fish, eggs, or tofu
correct_answer: Grilled chicken, fish, eggs, or tofu
explanation: Grilled, baked, or roasted chicken, fish, turkey, lean beef, eggs, low-fat cheese, and tofu are your recovery and muscle-building squad.
fun_fact: How it's cooked matters — grilled and baked beat fried every time.

question: What do healthy fats like avocado, nuts, and olive oil do for you?
explanation: Healthy fats are your immunity and flavor boosters. Salmon, nuts, seeds, olive oil, avocado, and nut butters keep you healthy and make food taste great.
fun_fact: Dietitians literally label fats 'immunity/flavor-boosting foods' on the performance plate.

question: Which drinks make the performance plate's hydration list?
option_a: Water, low-fat milk, and 100% fruit juice
correct_answer: Water, low-fat milk, and 100% fruit juice
explanation: Every performance plate includes fluids: water, low-fat milk (dairy or soy), or 100% fruit juice. Hydration is a component, not an afterthought.
fun_fact: Milk pulls double duty — hydration plus protein and calcium.

question: What's a hard-training-day dinner that hits all 5 components?
option_a: Whole-grain pasta with lean meatballs, broccoli, salad with oil dressing, and milk
correct_answer: Whole-grain pasta with lean meatballs, broccoli, salad with oil dressing, and milk
explanation: That plate has it all: grains for energy, lean protein for muscle, veggies for antioxidants, oil-based dressing for healthy fat, and milk to hydrate. Championship dinner.
fun_fact: Antioxidant-rich foods like broccoli and berries support recovery and immune function.

question: How often should an athlete eat to keep energy steady?
explanation: Consistent fuel = consistent energy. Eat every 2-3 hours with meals and smart snacks, and you'll feel the difference in class AND at practice.
fun_fact: Athletes with consistent intake have more energy and perform better all day long.

question: What makes a snack a SMART snack?
explanation: Pair them up and win: Greek yogurt + fruit, jerky + pretzels, hummus + crackers, milk + cereal. Carbs restore energy, protein rebuilds muscle.
fun_fact: A fruit smoothie with protein powder counts as a smart snack you can drink.

question: You have early morning training. What's the pre-session move?
option_a: A quick, easily digestible carb source
correct_answer: A quick, easily digestible carb source
explanation: Quick carbs before early training — toast, a banana, cereal — support your energy and performance without weighing you down.
fun_fact: Some athletes prefer carb-rich fluids like smoothies when solid food feels heavy early.

question: How soon after training should you eat a real meal?
option_b: Whenever you get around to it
explanation: Aim for a meal within an hour after training: carb-rich foods, high-quality protein, and plenty of fluids. Can't get a meal? A recovery snack starts the process.
fun_fact: Recovery starts the moment training ends — the sooner you fuel, the sooner you rebuild.

question: When should you NOT experiment with new foods?
option_a: Before big training sessions or competition
option_b: On a rest day at home
correct_answer: Before big training sessions or competition
explanation: Game day is not taste-test day. Know what sits well with your body, and save new foods for regular days. Compete on proven fuel.
fun_fact: Pro athletes lock in their competition-day menu weeks ahead — zero surprises.

question: What's the smart way to stay fueled on a packed school day?
option_a: Pack portable performance foods in your backpack
correct_answer: Pack portable performance foods in your backpack
explanation: Plan ahead like a pro. Portable snacks in your bag mean you can refuel every 2-3 hours between class, practice, and everything else.
fun_fact: Trail mix, jerky, fruit, and granola bars survive a school bag all day.

question: Where do the freshest, least-processed foods live in the grocery store?
explanation: Shop the perimeter first: fresh produce, meats, and dairy. The center aisles have their wins too, but the edges are where fresh lives.
fun_fact: Your grocery cart should look like your plate — full of different colors.

question: What's the Rule of 3s for choosing cereal?
explanation: Read the label like a pro: a performance cereal brings at least 3 grams of fiber and 3 grams of protein per serving.
fun_fact: For bread: 3g fiber per slice, whole grain first on the ingredient list, and no sugar in the first five ingredients.

question: Which yogurt should a performance-minded athlete grab?
explanation: Greek yogurt delivers roughly three times more protein than regular yogurt. Same aisle, triple the muscle support.
fun_fact: Eggs are the cheapest high-quality protein in the entire store.

question: What should you look for when buying lean meat?
explanation: Cuts named 'round' or 'loin,' less marbling, and 90% lean or higher — that's how you shop for muscle fuel.
fun_fact: A rotisserie chicken is the ultimate shortcut: quick, easy, ready-to-eat protein.

question: Which milk swap actually matches cow's milk for protein?
explanation: If you're skipping dairy, soy milk holds its own on protein. Almond milk brings only about 1 gram per cup — not enough to fuel recovery.
fun_fact: Cow's and soy milk each pack roughly 8x the protein of almond milk.

question: What are the 3 F's of buying produce?
explanation: Fresh and Frozen First! Frozen produce is just as nutrient-packed as fresh and won't spoil — stock that freezer with confidence.
fun_fact: Buying produce in season saves money AND tastes better.

question: What's a 60-second recovery meal you can make with a microwave?
option_a: Egg and cheese sandwich in a mug
correct_answer: Egg and cheese sandwich in a mug
explanation: Whisk an egg in a mug, microwave 60 seconds, slide it onto a whole wheat English muffin with cheese. Real fuel, faster than a drive-thru.
fun_fact: Burrito bowls with minute rice, canned beans, and rotisserie chicken take under 5 minutes.

question: When should you eat your full pre-training meal?
explanation: Give your body time to turn food into fuel — a full meal 3-4 hours out. Fuel for the work you EXPECT to do: bigger session, bigger fuel.
fun_fact: Elite athletes plan meals around training the way they plan practice drills.

question: What's the move 30-60 minutes before training?
option_a: A carb snack to top off your energy stores
correct_answer: A carb snack to top off your energy stores
explanation: Top off the tank! A carb-focused snack — banana, pretzels, granola bar — 30-60 minutes out primes your muscles with ready-to-use energy.
fun_fact: The harder the session ahead, the more carb-focused your pre-fuel should be.

question: Practice just ended. What's your recovery window move?
explanation: The first 30 minutes kick off recovery. Carbs refill your energy tank, protein rebuilds muscle. Chocolate milk, yogurt with granola, or a turkey wrap — then a full meal within a few hours.
fun_fact: Recovery fueling matters most when you train twice in one day.

question: How can you judge your training intensity without any equipment?
explanation: Easy: you can talk AND sing. Medium: you can talk but not sing. High: only a few words at a time. Match your fuel to your intensity like a pro.
fun_fact: Higher intensity = more carb-focused pre-fuel. Easy days can go balanced with protein and fats.

question: Your workout is under 45 minutes. Do you need carbs DURING it?
explanation: Short sessions run on the fuel you already have. Save the mid-workout carbs and sports drinks for longer sessions — that's when they pay off.
fun_fact: For long endurance training, athletes fuel every hour DURING the session to keep blood sugar stable.

question: What happens 3 days before a big competition?
option_a: Carbs go UP to load your muscles with energy
correct_answer: Carbs go UP to load your muscles with energy
explanation: Championship move: about three days out, shift to a high-carb performance plate. Your muscles stock up on stored energy so you hit peak power on game day.
fun_fact: Athletes call this glycogen 'supercompensation' — a fully loaded energy tank at the start line.

question: What does 'periodization' mean in fueling?
option_a: Adjusting your fuel to match your training — by day, week, and season
option_c: Only eating during your sport's season
correct_answer: Adjusting your fuel to match your training — by day, week, and season
explanation: Fuel for the work required. Your nutrition shifts with your training day, your training week, and your season — pre-season, in-season, championship taper, and offseason each get their own game plan.
fun_fact: In-season priority: fuel to perform AND recover. Offseason: recover, reset, and rebuild.
